title: Microsoft's Secure Boot has been broken for a decade and no one noticed until now
gist: ESET researchers found that Microsoft failed to revoke 11 known-defective UEFI 'shim' firmware images (some dating to 2013) that let attackers with novice-level skill completely bypass Secure Boot on both Windows and Linux devices; Microsoft only revoked them in its June 2026 patch after ESET's disclosure, and a firmware security expert calls the incident a rebuke of the entire Secure Boot trust model.

themes.theme: Secure Boot bypass via unrevoked shims

insights.claim: ESET researchers identified 11 UEFI 'shim' firmware images, at least one dating to 2013, that were known to be defective/vulnerable but remained digitally signed and trusted by Microsoft, meaning Secure Boot has been trivially bypassable for 13 of its 14 years since introduction in 2012.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: A near-universal, long-lived bypass of a foundational security control affecting virtually all Windows and many Linux devices represents a major latent enterprise security liability; IT security vendors and Microsoft itself face scrutiny over patch-management governance.
insights.locator: opening paragraph, industry-wide standard Microsoft invented

insights.claim: ESET researcher Martin Smolár states the bypass requires no novel vulnerability discovery — only an old, still-trusted, unrevoked shim binary and basic knowledge of how UEFI shims work — making it exploitable by novice hackers.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Low attacker skill floor dramatically raises real-world exploitation risk versus a typical zero-day, meaning the threat is not hypothetical but immediately actionable by low-sophistication actors once the technique is public.
insights.quote: An attacker needs no complicated exploitation primitives—only a copy of an old, still-trusted, but unrevoked shim binary and a basic understanding of how UEFI shims work. That is enough to bypass such an essential security feature as UEFI Secure Boot.
insights.locator: quote from ESET researcher Martin Smolár

insights.claim: A successful Secure Boot bypass lets attackers with physical access install persistent malicious firmware (bootkits) that load before the OS and survive OS reinstallation or hard drive replacement, in the same class as known nation-state bootkits: LoJax (Russia, 2018), MosaicRegressor (2020), CosmicStrand (2022), and BlackLotus (2023).
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Ties the newly disclosed weakness to a documented lineage of real-world state-sponsored bootkit campaigns, indicating sophisticated threat actors have strong incentive and prior capability to exploit exactly this class of flaw.
insights.locator: Without Secure Boot, attackers with brief physical access

insights.claim: Microsoft finally revoked the 11 defective shims in its regular monthly patch release in June 2026, only after ESET brought them to CERT's and Microsoft's attention; some shims had gone unrevoked for over a decade despite Microsoft being the party responsible for shim signing and revocation.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated patch action item (June 2026 update) that enterprise IT and security teams must confirm is deployed; unpatched fleets remain exposed to a now-public bypass technique.
insights.locator: The company finally revoked them in its regular monthly patch release in June

insights.claim: The dbx (revoked certificates/hashes) database used in UEFI Secure Boot is limited to only 32kb of space, which is insufficient to list every Linux boot component individually, forcing Microsoft to rely on separate, more complex version-based revocation mechanisms (SBAT and Secure Boot SVN) that appear to have been mismanaged, contributing to the decade-long gap.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural, not merely operational, root cause (storage/architecture constraints of the dbx mechanism) suggesting the failure could recur unless Microsoft redesigns the revocation architecture rather than just patching individual shims.
insights.locator: Given the high number of Linux components executed during bootup

insights.claim: Firmware security expert HD Moore, CEO and founder of runZero and a longtime Secure Boot critic, says the incident is 'a solid rebuke of the entire secure boot model,' citing Microsoft's role as the de facto single root of trust for the whole UEFI platform, the mechanism's inability to scale, and components' ability to keep booting even after top-level certificates expire.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: An independent, credentialed security expert calling for a fundamental redesign ('the whole ecosystem... needs a reboot') signals this is not a one-off patch issue but a structural weakness in a control relied upon industry-wide, which is likely to draw regulatory and enterprise procurement scrutiny of Microsoft's UEFI trust governance.
insights.quote: The whole ecosystem is somewhat broken and needs a reboot.
insights.locator: quote from HD Moore, firmware security expert, CEO and founder of runZero

insights.claim: Even the expiration of the Microsoft certificate that signed the vulnerable shims, which occurred late last month (June 2026), was not by itself sufficient to revoke the affected shims, requiring separate explicit revocation action.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Reveals that certificate expiration is not a reliable automatic safety net in the Secure Boot trust chain, meaning organizations cannot assume routine certificate lifecycle events close security gaps and must track explicit revocation lists.
insights.locator: Further complicating the process, even the expiration of the Microsoft certificate

insights.claim: Remediation guidance: Windows users who have installed Microsoft's June 2026 update batch are no longer vulnerable (except default-configured Windows 11 Secured-core PCs, which were likely already protected); Linux users should check the Linux Vendor Firmware Service or their distributor, and revocation status can be checked with the uefi-dbx-audit script.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Actionable remediation checklist for enterprise IT/security teams to verify exposure and confirm patch status immediately rather than assuming automatic protection.
